worth_it_explanation: Account structure is the frame every other setting hangs on. When distinct goals or brands share one container, budgets cannot be steered, Smart Bidding learns from a blended signal, and reporting hides which part of the account is working. The check is a review of how campaigns and ad groups are organized, with no rebuild required to see the problem. The fix is a structural change to plan rather than a setting to flip, so catching it early saves a costly reorganization later.
intro: A Google Ads account is organized in three layers: the account, its campaigns, and the ad groups inside each campaign. Campaigns hold the budget and the bidding, and ad groups hold the ads and keywords that share a theme. When that structure does not match how the business thinks about its goals and budgets, control and reporting both suffer. These checks confirm that distinct goals are separated where they need their own budget and bidding, and that ad groups stay tightly themed so the ads can match the search.

audit_checks.title: Campaigns with distinct goals or budgets are not separated
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Budget and bidding live at the campaign level. When prospecting and remarketing, or brand and non-brand, share one campaign, the account cannot give more budget to the stronger goal or set a different target for it, so spend flows to whatever the algorithm favors rather than to the priority.

educate.base:
In Google Ads, the campaign is where budget and bid strategy are set. Every ad group inside a campaign draws from the same daily budget and is steered by the same bid strategy and target. That makes the campaign the unit of control: two goals that need different budgets, different targets, or different reporting belong in different campaigns.

When distinct goals share one campaign, the account loses that control. Brand search, where someone already typing the company name converts cheaply, mixed with non-brand prospecting in the same campaign means the cheap brand conversions pull the average cost down and the shared bid strategy spends toward them, starving the prospecting that actually finds new customers. The same problem appears when a prospecting goal and a remarketing goal share a campaign. Remarketing converts at a far lower cost and absorbs the budget.

A few signs point to goals that need separating:
  • Brand and non-brand keywords sit in the same campaign, so brand traffic inflates the campaign's reported performance
  • Prospecting and remarketing run together, so the budget drifts toward the cheaper remarketing conversions
  • Two product lines with different margins or targets share one budget, so the account cannot fund them independently
Separating goals into their own campaigns lets each carry its own budget, target, and reporting. You may want to confirm which goals the business funds and measures separately before deciding where the campaign boundaries should fall.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, Shopping or Performance Max for the catalog and Search for non-brand queries serve different goals and often warrant separate campaigns so the catalog spend and the query spend can be budgeted apart. You may want to confirm that high-margin and low-margin product lines are not sharing a single budget that the algorithm spends toward the easier sales.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation account, brand defense and non-brand prospecting convert at very different costs, so sharing one campaign lets cheap brand leads flatter the average. You may want to separate brand and non-brand into their own campaigns so the prospecting budget is protected.

investigate.intro: Review the campaign list to confirm that goals needing separate budgets or targets are in separate campaigns, not combined.

google_ads_ui.label: Google Ads UI
steps: Open the Campaigns view and list the campaigns with their budgets and bid strategies.
For each campaign, open its ad groups and note whether it mixes goals that the business funds or measures separately, for example brand and non-brand keywords, or prospecting and remarketing.
Where one campaign carries more than one such goal, record it. A campaign whose budget cannot be aimed at the priority goal because a second goal shares it is the pattern to flag.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per campaign, whether it mixes goals that the business funds or measures separately. A campaign holding a single goal, or goals that genuinely share a budget and target, passes. A campaign combining goals that need independent budgets or targets is a fail, recorded with the campaign name and the goals that should be split.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record, for each campaign, whether it combines goals that need separate budgets or targets to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s account combines goals that need separate budgets or targets inside a single campaign. Because budget and bidding are set at the campaign level, the account cannot fund or aim the priority goal independently, so spend drifts toward whichever conversions are cheapest rather than toward the goal that matters. {{voice_recommend}} splitting the combined goals into their own campaigns so each carries its own budget, target, and reporting, then watching that the priority goal holds its spend after the change.

audit_checks.title: Ad groups mix unrelated keyword themes
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When one ad group holds keywords from several themes, the ads cannot speak to all of them, so the ad shown rarely matches the search. The weaker match lowers relevance and click-through, which raises cost per click and can cap how often the ads show.

educate.base:
An ad group holds a set of keywords and the ads that serve them. The ads in an ad group rotate against every keyword in it, so an ad can only match the search well when the keywords share a theme. When one ad group mixes unrelated themes, no single ad fits them all, and the search sees an ad written for a different intent.

Google's current guidance favors tightly themed ad groups over the older single-keyword approach, but tightly themed still means one intent per ad group, not many. An ad group that holds running shoes, hiking boots, and sandals cannot show an ad that speaks to all three, so the running-shoe searcher may see the generic footwear ad and click through less often. Lower click-through feeds a lower Ad Strength and Quality Score, which raises the cost to show.

A few signs point to ad groups that hold too many themes:
  • The keywords in one ad group span clearly different products or intents
  • The ad headlines are generic because no specific headline could fit every keyword
  • Click-through rate is low across the ad group despite reasonable impressions
Regrouping keywords so each ad group carries one theme lets the ads match the search. You may want to confirm the intent behind each keyword before regrouping. Match type and search-term data can reveal themes the keyword text alone hides.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, ad groups built from a flat keyword dump often span whole departments. You may want to align ad groups with the site's category structure so each one carries a single product theme and its ads can name that product.

learn_more.source_title: Organize your account with ad groups

investigate.intro: Confirm that each ad group's keywords share one theme and that the ads can speak to that theme.

google_ads_ui.label: Google Ads UI
steps: Open a campaign and review each ad group's keyword list.
Note any ad group whose keywords span clearly different products or intents, for example several unrelated categories in one group.
Open the ads in those ad groups and check whether a single ad could match every keyword. An ad group where the ads must stay generic to fit the mixed keywords is the pattern to flag.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per ad group, whether its keywords share one theme. An ad group whose keywords share a theme and whose ads can speak to it passes. An ad group mixing unrelated themes, where the ads cannot match every keyword, is a fail recorded with the ad group name and the themes that should be split.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record, for each ad group, whether its keywords share a single theme to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} ad groups on {{site_name}} that mix unrelated keyword themes, so no single ad can match every keyword in the group. The mismatched ad lowers relevance and click-through, which raises the cost to show and can limit how often the ads appear. {{voice_recommend}} regrouping the keywords so each ad group carries one theme, then writing ads that speak to that theme, and confirming click-through improves once the ads and keywords align.
